title: Whether childhood homes should be preserved
summary: Argue whether the childhood homes of significant individuals — writers, musicians, scientists, leaders — should be preserved as heritage sites or allowed to be demolished or repurposed.
topic: Whether childhood homes should be preserved
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) on whether childhood homes of historically significant individuals should be preserved as heritage sites. Consider what is actually gained by preserving a building versus what it costs in terms of resources, urban development, and the risk of misrepresenting a life. You may refer to real examples such as the preservation of Shakespeare's birthplace in Stratford-upon-Avon, Nelson Mandela's house in Soweto, or the debate over Emily Dickinson's Amherst home. Use a cleft sentence, a concession, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: Shakespeare's birthplace on Henley Street in Stratford-upon-Avon attracts nearly a million visitors a year…
What a preserved childhood home offers is not access to the past but a carefully managed encounter with…
Granted, some buildings genuinely illuminate the conditions in which a person was formed…
The question is whether it is the building that matters or the idea the building has come to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: di sản / địa điểm di sản
được chỉ định / được công nhận
tái sử dụng cho mục đích khác
công trình được bảo hộ / công trình trong danh sách bảo tồn
làm sạch / tẩy trắng lịch sử
bị đóng băng trong thời gian

hintVocab.phrase: to be reduced to a symbol
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị thu gọn thành biểu tượng
lựa chọn của người quản lý bảo tàng

sampleText:
Shakespeare's birthplace on Henley Street in Stratford-upon-Avon attracts nearly a million visitors a year. They walk through the low-ceilinged rooms, inspect the furniture attributed to the Shakespeare family, and stand in the garden where, it is implied, something essential about the playwright was formed. Whether any of this is actually true is a matter scholars have disputed for centuries. The birthplace has been restored, rebuilt in parts, and extensively redecorated since the sixteenth century. What visitors encounter is not Shakespeare's childhood but a carefully managed version of it.

This is the central problem with preserving childhood homes as heritage sites. What is being offered is rarely the past itself but an interpretation of the past, constructed with the benefit of knowing how the story ends. The house in which Nelson Mandela grew up in Soweto was preserved precisely because Mandela became who he became — the same house, had he lived an ordinary life, would have been demolished long ago. The decision about which homes are worth preserving is always made retrospectively, which means it is always shaped by our knowledge of the person's later significance rather than by the actual conditions of the childhood itself.

Granted, some buildings do illuminate history in ways that written accounts cannot. Mandela's Soweto home — a small, modest township house — communicates something immediately and powerfully about the conditions in which apartheid South Africa was lived. The house is not merely a symbol; it is evidence. Similarly, visiting Emily Dickinson's Amherst home allows readers to understand the physical constraints — the small bedroom, the view from the window, the distance from the street — that shaped the geography of her imagination. These are genuine insights that a photograph or a biography would not convey with the same force.

Even so, preservation is not neutral. A house that is maintained, staffed, and curated is a house that is being argued about, and the argument is never fully disclosed. Rooms are staged; objects are selected; narratives are chosen and others are excluded. The result is often a figure reduced to a symbol — inspirational, uncomplicated, and stripped of the contradictions that made them interesting. What is preserved, in many cases, is not a life but a version of a life that serves a particular purpose.

A commemorative plaque on an ordinary wall can acknowledge a connection without freezing it. The building beneath the plaque can go on being used, changed, and inhabited — which is, after all, what buildings are for. Selective preservation of the most genuinely significant and well-evidenced sites, combined with honest interpretation, is preferable to heritage-as-myth.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to expose what heritage sites actually offer
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What visitors encounter is not Shakespeare's childhood but a carefully managed version of it' dùng cấu trúc 'What X encounters is not A but B' để phơi bày khoảng cách giữa kỳ vọng của du khách (tuổi thơ thực sự) và những gì thực tế được cung cấp (một phiên bản được quản lý) — đây là cú 'bật ngược' sắc nét đặt luận điểm trung tâm của bài ngay từ đầu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What visitors encounter is not Shakespeare's childhood but a carefully managed version of it' uses a what-cleft to expose the gap between what visitors expect (the real past) and what is actually offered (a curated reconstruction), setting up the essay's central argument from the first paragraph.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive perfect to suggest gradual, undisclosed change
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'has been restored, rebuilt in parts, and extensively redecorated since the sixteenth century' dùng hoàn thành bị động với ba động từ liên tiếp để ám chỉ rằng ngôi nhà đã trải qua nhiều thay đổi tích lũy theo thời gian mà không cần đặt tên cho bất kỳ ai chịu trách nhiệm — điều này củng cố lập luận rằng 'tính xác thực' là một ảo tưởng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'has been restored, rebuilt in parts, and extensively redecorated' uses a passive perfect series to imply accumulated change over centuries without naming those responsible, reinforcing the essay's argument that the building's claimed authenticity is constructed, not inherited.

sampleTranslationVi:
Nơi sinh của Shakespeare trên phố Henley ở Stratford-upon-Avon thu hút gần một triệu du khách mỗi năm. Họ bước qua những căn phòng trần thấp, kiểm tra đồ nội thất được cho là của gia đình Shakespeare, và đứng trong khu vườn nơi, theo ý được ngụ ý, điều gì đó thiết yếu về tác giả kịch đã được hình thành. Liệu điều nào trong số này có thực sự đúng hay không là vấn đề mà các học giả đã tranh luận trong nhiều thế kỷ. Nơi sinh đã được phục hồi, xây dựng lại từng phần và trang trí lại rộng rãi kể từ thế kỷ thứ mười sáu. Điều du khách gặp gỡ không phải là tuổi thơ của Shakespeare mà là một phiên bản được quản lý cẩn thận.

Đây là vấn đề trung tâm khi bảo tồn nhà tuổi thơ như di sản văn hóa. Điều đang được cung cấp hiếm khi là quá khứ thực sự mà là một cách diễn giải về quá khứ, được xây dựng với lợi thế của việc biết câu chuyện kết thúc như thế nào. Ngôi nhà Nelson Mandela lớn lên ở Soweto được bảo tồn chính xác vì Mandela đã trở thành người như ông — cùng ngôi nhà đó, nếu ông sống một cuộc đời bình thường, đã bị phá hủy từ lâu. Quyết định về ngôi nhà nào đáng được bảo tồn luôn được đưa ra một cách hồi tố, có nghĩa là nó luôn được định hình bởi kiến thức của chúng ta về tầm quan trọng sau này của người đó hơn là bởi các điều kiện thực tế của tuổi thơ đó.

Công nhận, một số tòa nhà thực sự chiếu sáng lịch sử theo những cách mà các tài khoản bằng văn bản không thể. Ngôi nhà ở Soweto của Mandela — một ngôi nhà khu phố nhỏ khiêm tốn — truyền đạt điều gì đó ngay lập tức và mạnh mẽ về những điều kiện sống ở Nam Phi thời apartheid. Ngôi nhà không chỉ là biểu tượng; đó là bằng chứng. Tương tự, việc thăm ngôi nhà Amherst của Emily Dickinson cho phép độc giả hiểu những hạn chế vật lý — phòng ngủ nhỏ, tầm nhìn từ cửa sổ, khoảng cách với con phố — đã định hình địa lý của trí tưởng tượng của cô ấy. Đây là những hiểu biết thực sự mà một bức ảnh hay tiểu sử sẽ không truyền đạt với cùng sức mạnh.

Dẫu vậy, bảo tồn không trung lập. Một ngôi nhà được bảo trì, có nhân viên và được quản lý là một ngôi nhà đang được tranh luận, và cuộc tranh luận không bao giờ được tiết lộ đầy đủ. Các phòng được dàn dựng; đồ vật được chọn lọc; câu chuyện được chọn và những câu chuyện khác bị loại trừ. Kết quả thường là một nhân vật bị thu gọn thành biểu tượng — truyền cảm hứng, đơn giản và bị tước bỏ những mâu thuẫn khiến họ thú vị. Điều được bảo tồn, trong nhiều trường hợp, không phải là một cuộc đời mà là một phiên bản của một cuộc đời phục vụ một mục đích cụ thể.

Một biển tưởng niệm trên một bức tường thông thường có thể thừa nhận sự kết nối mà không cần đóng băng nó. Tòa nhà bên dưới tấm biển có thể tiếp tục được sử dụng, thay đổi và có người ở — đó là, suy cho cùng, những gì các tòa nhà là để làm. Bảo tồn có chọn lọc những địa điểm quan trọng và được chứng minh tốt nhất, kết hợp với cách diễn giải trung thực, được ưu tiên hơn di sản như huyền thoại.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to expose the gap between expectation and reality at heritage sites?
options: Visitors to Shakespeare's birthplace do not find the real past.
What visitors encounter is not Shakespeare's childhood but a carefully managed version of it.
It is the visitors who encounter what Shakespeare's childhood was like in his birthplace.
Shakespeare's childhood is not what visitors find when they go to Stratford.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What visitors encounter is not A but B' uses a cleft to pivot from expected content (the real past) to actual content (a managed version), setting up the essay's central argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What X encounters is not A but B'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive perfect series implies accumulated, undisclosed change?
options: Restorers restored, rebuilt, and redecorated the house over the centuries.
The house has been restored, rebuilt in parts, and extensively redecorated since the sixteenth century.
The house was being restored, rebuilt, and redecorated since the sixteenth century.
The sixteenth century house is restored, rebuilt, and redecorated by various people.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'has been restored, rebuilt in parts, and extensively redecorated' is a present perfect passive series that implies multiple changes over centuries without naming those responsible.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses the present perfect passive with three past participles?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'authenticity' mean in the context of heritage sites?
options: The legal status of a building as protected by heritage law
The genuine, original character of a place — whether it truly represents the historical period claimed
The number of visitors a site attracts each year
The accuracy of the guidebooks and information provided to tourists
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Authenticity' at heritage sites refers to whether a place genuinely preserves or represents the original — the essay argues this is often compromised by reconstruction and curation.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'authentic' as 'genuinely original' rather than 'restored to look original'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'constructed with the benefit of knowing how the story ends'?
options: Heritage buildings are designed by architects who specialise in historical accuracy.
The decision to preserve a building is always made after the person has become famous, so the significance is read backwards.
Historical narratives are more accurate when they are written after the events they describe.
Visitors understand more about a figure's life when they already know the full biography.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that which homes are preserved is decided retrospectively — we know the person became significant, and that knowledge shapes how we interpret and present the building.
mcQuestions.hint: The key word is 'retrospectively' — preserved because of what we already know, not what the building itself shows.

mcQuestions.prompt: What concession does the essay make about childhood home preservation?
options: All childhood homes of significant figures deserve equal preservation funding.
Some buildings genuinely illuminate history in ways written accounts cannot, providing tangible, evidential insight.
Preservation is always preferable to demolition because buildings cannot be rebuilt once lost.
The problem of preservation is unsolvable because historians always disagree.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay concedes that some buildings — like Mandela's Soweto house and Dickinson's Amherst home — do offer genuine historical insight that written sources cannot convey.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the paragraph beginning with 'Granted' for the conceded points.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay cite Mandela's Soweto house as a positive example?
options: Because it is the only childhood home in South Africa to have been preserved
Because it communicates immediately and powerfully the conditions of life under apartheid
Because Mandela himself requested that it be preserved
Because it is the most architecturally significant building in Soweto
mcQuestions.explanation: The Mandela house is cited as a case where the building itself is evidence — its scale and modesty communicate social reality in a way no photograph or text can fully replicate.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay says the house is 'not merely a symbol; it is evidence' — what kind of evidence?

options: To make historical buildings physically clean and safe for visitors
To present a simplified, uncritical version of history that removes difficult truths
To use modern methods to date and verify historical artefacts
To translate historical documents into contemporary language
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To sanitise history' means to clean up or simplify the historical record by removing contradictions, failures, or uncomfortable truths — leaving only an idealised version.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of sanitising as making something 'safe' — but safe from what, in this context?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay argue about 'preservation' being 'not neutral'?
options: Preservation requires political decisions about which communities have the most cultural influence.
Every preserved building reflects curatorial choices — what is shown, what is excluded, how the person is interpreted — and those choices are never fully transparent.
Preservation is not neutral because it always costs more money than demolition.
Preserved buildings are not neutral spaces because visitors bring their own assumptions.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that preserving a house involves staging rooms, selecting objects, and constructing a narrative — choices that shape how a person is remembered and are rarely made explicit to visitors.
mcQuestions.hint: The paragraph after 'Even so' explains what 'not neutral' means — look for what is staged, selected, and excluded.

mcQuestions.prompt: What alternative to full preservation does the essay suggest?
options: Demolishing all childhood homes and replacing them with modern museums
A commemorative plaque on a building that is otherwise allowed to remain in use
Converting childhood homes into public libraries
Creating virtual reality reconstructions instead of preserving physical buildings
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay suggests that a commemorative plaque on a living building acknowledges the connection without 'freezing' the building — allowing it to continue being used and changed.
mcQuestions.hint: The final paragraph proposes a middle way — what does it involve?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means 'to make something simpler and more heroic than it actually was'?
options: to contextualise
to mythologise
to designate
to repurpose
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To mythologise' means to turn something into a myth — to simplify and romanticise it beyond its actual complexity.
mcQuestions.hint: A myth is a simplified, elevated story — to mythologise is to create that kind of story.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'made retrospectively' mean in the context of preservation decisions?
options: Made immediately after a person's birth to preserve their childhood environment
Made after the person has died and their significance is already established
Made by a committee that reviews historical evidence before deciding
Made by the person themselves during their lifetime
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Retrospectively' means looking back — preservation decisions are always made after the fact, once the person's importance is known, which distorts what the building is said to represent.
mcQuestions.hint: The prefix 'retro-' means 'backward' or 'in the past'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition signals a pivot back to the main argument after the concession?
options: Granted,
In conclusion,
Even so, preservation is not neutral.
Similarly,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, preservation is not neutral' acknowledges the conceded value of some preservation and pivots back to the essay's critical argument about what preservation costs.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the transition that follows the concession paragraph and introduces a counter-argument.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains a consistent B2 formal register?
options: Shakespeare's house is basically a fake tourist trap tbh, not worth visiting.
Selective preservation of the most genuinely significant and well-evidenced sites, combined with honest interpretation, is preferable to heritage-as-myth.
Old houses of famous people should be kept or not, it depends on the situation.
Preserving childhood homes is ok sometimes but also not really necessary in all cases.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses precise vocabulary ('genuinely significant', 'well-evidenced', 'honest interpretation'), a nominative contrast ('heritage-as-myth'), and a measured, analytical tone.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid hedging, colloquialisms, and vague qualifiers like 'basically' or 'kind of'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is a 'tangible connection' in the context of heritage sites?
options: A financial link between a museum and its government funders
A physical, concrete sense of contact with a historical person through the actual place
A documented relationship between two historical figures
A legal agreement between a heritage organisation and a property owner
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tangible' means physically real, able to be touched or experienced directly. A 'tangible connection' to the past means feeling a real, physical link rather than an abstract one.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word correctly completes: 'The house has been _____ by the local authority as a protected building'?
options: demolished
mythologised
designated
repurposed
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Designated' is the correct word for the formal act of giving something official status (as a heritage site, a national park, etc.).
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'designate' as 'officially name or classify'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a conditional to speculate about an alternative history?
options: The same house, had he lived an ordinary life, would have been demolished long ago.
The same house, if he would have lived an ordinary life, would demolish long ago.
The same house, had he lived an ordinary life, will be demolished long ago.
The same house, if he had lived an ordinary life, demolishes long ago.
mcQuestions.explanation: An inverted past conditional ('had he lived …') signals a hypothetical that did not happen. The main clause uses 'would have been' + past participle for the counterfactual result.
mcQuestions.hint: Inverted conditional: 'Had + subject + past participle, … would have + past participle.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a place — a school, a neighbourhood, a building — that shaped who you are? If it were demolished tomorrow, what would be lost? Would it matter to anyone other than you, and why?
